Patch Set 5: (1 inline comment)

The concept of lease is unclear to me. Few questions:

- Exclusive open is not enforced. It means "tell me if someone else open the file", it will not prevent other from opening the file, "exclusive" means "warn me" but not "block the other ones". Correct?

- Apart exclusive open, what will be the other use cases of "lease"?

Patch Set 5:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 5: (1 inline comment)

The concept of lease is unclear to me. Few questions:
Exclusive open is not enforced. It means "tell me if someone else open the file", it will not prevent other from opening the file, "exclusive" means "warn me" but not "block the other ones". Correct?

Correct. Lease won't block normal open.
From the implementation of linux, ideally the lease holder should be notified and must release the lease in a limited time before normal open continues. 

Apart exclusive open, what will be the other use cases of "lease"?

Read lease for example. In that case, only Write open will break the lease.

Patch Set 7: Verified

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/5990f884-de44-11e2-b04c-52540035b04c).  Ran 15 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 7:

>Correct. Lease won't block normal open. From the implementation of linux, ideally the lease holder should be notified and must release the lease in a limited time before normal open continues.

>Read lease for example. In that case, only Write open will break the lease.

But what you're describing is close to current LDLM behaviour. What's the interest of this lease mechanism compared to it? user-space possibilities? exclusive open?
There is no callback for "lease", that means that lease holder will detect a lease has been removed late.

Patch Set 7:

"But what you're describing is close to current LDLM behaviour. What's the interest of this lease mechanism compared to it? user-space possibilities? exclusive open? There is no callback for "lease", that means that lease holder will detect a lease has been removed late."

Yes, pretty much it's the same mechanism as DLM lock. We use open lock for the implementation actually, as you can see.

For the IT_RELEASE, you have to rebase your patch to this one, also I think the exclusive open will be updated to fit into IT_RELEASE usage. This patch should have been worked out before IT_RELEASE.

For example, to use ll_lease_open() for IT_RELEASE, we need to pass in IT_RELEASE_OPEN as intent operation so it will do the exactly same thing in your patch.
At the close phase, we have to pack the open lock handle into the close request so that we can detect if lease is broken at server side.

If it's okay for you, I can work out a draft patch for IT_RELEASE to use lease mechanism in this patch.

Patch Set 7:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/be27671a-de76-11e2-afb2-52540035b04c).  Ran 17 tests.  1 tests failed: runtests.

Patch Set 7:

Jinshan, the behavior of this isn't quite what is described in the commit message:

 # cd /mnt/lustre
 # touch 0
 # multiop 0 oO_RDWR:eRE+_eU &
 [1] 19755
 FMODE_READ lease(1) has applied.

 # cd /mnt/lustre2
 # multiop 0 oO_RDWR:eRE+_eU
 multiop: apply lease error: Device or resource busy
 # multiop 0 oO_RDWR:eWE+_eU
 multiop: apply lease error: Device or resource busy

Patch Set 7:

Only exclusive open is supported right now, so even the file is opened with the same mode, the 2nd try will be denied.

I'll fix the comment in the next commit.

Thank you very much for testing the patch.

Patch Set 8: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Jinshan, I admit it's a minor point, but I don't think you should ass och_inode. 1) For interoperability, I think we are stuck with cases where there is a valid och but no inode has been instantiated on the client. 2) Every caller of ll_lease_close() has the inode at hand and could pass it down, alleviating the need for the och_inode member.

Yes, fair enough. I will update my patch.

Patch Set 9: (2 inline comments)

Why do we 'steal' the och from lli and give it to the fd?

With the way we're setting fd->fd_omode now it has the same information as file->f_flags. Can't we just use file->f_flags and do away with fd_omode?

With the way we're setting fd->fd_omode now it has the same information as file->f_flags. Can't we just use file->f_flags and do away with fd_omode?

Why do we 'steal' the och from lli and give it to the fd?
With the way we're setting fd->fd_omode now it has the same information as file->f_flags. Can't we just use file->f_flags and do away with fd_omode?"

This piece of code has already been there for a long time. Is this really worth stopping land this patch?

Patch Set 10: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

I was really more interested in the first question. Sorry to be difficult, but I think that moving och around like this makes some already intricate code even more intricate. Can we kill fd_och, move fd_lease_och to ll_inode_info, and add a bit to fd_flags (LL_FILE_LEASE_OWNER) to signify that this file owns *lli->lli_lease_och?

Patch Set 10:

> John L. Hammond		12:29 AM
> I guess Fan Yong suggested this, but there are paths (jumps to out_child,...) in mdt_reint_open() that call mdt_object_open_unlock() but not mdt_object_open_lock().

Right. There are several paths that the mdt_object_open_unlock() called without mdt_object_open_lock() called firstly, then the operation on the  mdt_object::mot_open_sem maybe not match.
Patch Set 10: (5 inline comments)
Uploaded patch set 11.

Patch Set 10: (10 inline comments)

Resubmitted to address reviewers concerns.

This is way more involved than is required by HSM. We shouldn't have ioctls that are almost but not quite FS_SETLEASE. Any ioctls that we add of this form should be designed and reviewed. I think that these should not exist as they are, but we cannot easily unit test the lease functionality without them. The business of moving ochs from inode to file, and of adding data to the och is also troubling as we have some unfixed bugs and a fair amount of unpaid debt around open handles.

I you need to rebase please add those assert checks in mdt_object_free(). Also och_lease_handle it more descriptive than och_lockh.

Patch Set 13: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

(18 inline comments)

Lots of very minor style issues that could be corrected by this patch.

Most of the technical content I leave up to Oleg to comment on.

I do agree with John that I would feel a lot better if this implemented the standard Linux kernel lease mechanism with fcntl() so that we can also use this for Samba and other applications that might benefit from it, instead of having two nearly-the-same implementations that don't work together.

Unfortunately, I'm not in a position to comment on how easy/hard it would be to fix this discrepancy (both in this patch and in the HSM code that depends on it), but code duplication is always something to be avoided.

The main reason this patch might need a refresh (and all of the style issues to be fixed at that time) is because it looks like the test will introduce an interop failure with older servers.  Please verify either way.

parents.subject: LU-3340 mdt: HSM memory requests management

commit.subject: LU-2919 hsm: Implementation of exclusive open
commit.message:
LU-2919 hsm: Implementation of exclusive open

In this patch, a framework of lease is implemented. However,
only exclusive lease is supported right now.

To apply a lease, MDS_OPEN_LEASE must be set to open the file, EX
mode open lock is returned to the client side to hold a lease. From
that time on, if this file is opened again by other processes, the
open lock will be revoked so the client who holds the lease will
know the lease is already broken by checking that open lock.

To release a lease, normal close is used. The client will revoke the
open lock before sending CLOSE request.

Lease can be applied in two ways. ll_lease_open()/close() can be
called directly if the lease holder is in kernel space; or if the
lease holder lives in user space, it has to open the file first and
then use ioctl() with command LL_IOC_SET_LEASE to apply a lease. The
lease holder has to poll the lease status itself.

Signed-off-by: Jinshan Xiong <jinshan.xiong@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: John L. Hammond <john.hammond@intel.com>
Change-Id: I181edbb0b31fcf60858656170528d2ffda9c9bb6
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/6730
Tested-by: Hudson
Tested-by: Maloo <whamcloud.maloo@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
